---
title: "ai-minerals"
---

![](data/derived/portfolio_charts/region_locator_map.png){fig-alt="North America locator map showing the five regions worked across in this portfolio: Bear Cub (Nome, AK) for OCR + 3-D modeling of historical drill logs; and four regional MPM pipelines at Tanacross (eastern Alaska), the BC Golden Triangle, the California Mother Lode, and the Arizona porphyry-Cu belt."}

I have nearly thirty years of programming experience developing
anti-malware solutions on Windows, mostly low-level work on kernel
drivers and Windows internals. None of that has anything to do with
mining; this site is what I've been playing with in my off-hours.

Disclaimer: I'm not a geologist or a data scientist. But I
have a deep passion for mining.

A few years ago I started helping my mom research a book about her
grandfather, Walter W. Johnson, a mining and mechanical engineer and
builder of large placer-mining machinery. In the process we discovered that
the family still owned a large interest in several patented mining
claims in Nome, Alaska. One of those claims had recently been
high-graded by lessees of the other partial-interest holder without
our knowledge, and the others purportedly still held unmined gold.
As I started researching the book and the mining claims, two things
happened that I was not expecting. 1) I fell in love with mining.
2) Every mining problem I encountered looked like it could be
decomposed into a computer program. Thus began my quest
to apply my programming skills to the domain in every way I
could. Computing Bear Cub's resource estimates from 100-year-old drill
logs is one example of that.

The rest of this site is me poking around to see how useful public
data is for machine learning. One
offshoot of that line of investigation is goldbug, which unites
it with one of my other unexpected new passions: title research.
goldbug looks at machine learning through the lens of public land
law, showing you not just where the most gold might be, but where
the most gold that you can legally claim as your own might be.

KoBold Metals works in this part of the field commercially,
across four continents, with a stack that goes well beyond what
I'm doing here (full-physics inversions, Bayesian drill planning,
proprietary sensor data on top of the public layers). I've been
playing with what tree-based ML (random forests, gradient
boosting) trained on public data looks like at the scale of an
individual prospector or a family-owned claim.

## Chapters

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [1. Bear Cub (Cape Nome placer Au)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

After learning we still owned these claims, I had to figure out
what they were and what this meant to us. I dove into the history
first. Old newspapers, state and national
archives, museum collections; territorial court transcripts, recorded
documents, old mining journals, mining engineer reports, drill logs,
company reports. Bear Cub sits on the legendary Third Beach Line at
Nome, and cases involving its ownership had already worked their way
up to the Supreme Court within a few years of its 1899 staking.
Figuring out how to ingest all this disparate information, add
structure to it, and use it as input for computational processes is
turning into a lifelong quest. I tracked down the Bear Cub drill logs
in the archives of the Huntington Library, in their Charles Janin
collection: 24 early 20th-century placer churn drill logs. I
developed a multi-engine OCR pipeline (Tesseract, TrOCR, Cloud
Vision, Claude Opus arbitrating with structured-output JSON) to
ingest them, programmatically mapped each hole onto a recent plat of
the claim, built a 3D model of the bedrock and each hole, and used
standard algorithms (Voronoi-cell volumetrics, Monte Carlo
uncertainty) to compute potential yield. In the end, per-hole grades
matched two mining engineers' independent analyses within 6%. I'm
using the results to feed the damages calculation in our active
litigation, and to develop a plan to mine the deeper gold that's
still there. On top of the same 24-hole grid I also built a
Gaussian-process next-drill-site recommender to ask where hole #25
should go.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Bear Cub aerial photo with pay-zone grade overlay](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

[![Fence diagram: 24 Bear Cub drill holes, log-scale gold grade, pay-zone framing](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_fence.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)

[![Next-drill-site recommender on Bear Cub: max-variance + max-EI picks against the GP-predicted grade surface](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch1_bear_cub_gp.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html)
:::
::::

## Machine learning on public geological data

When I started looking at machine learning on public geological
data, I had two criteria: which regions interested me personally, and
which felt amenable to the methodology. My great-grandfather, and
his four sons including my grandfather, had mined across the
California Mother Lode, British Columbia, and Alaska, so those areas
were the natural draw. The Arizona copper porphyry belt held no
personal interest for me, but the data and the geology looked right
for the methodology to work.

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [2. Regional MPM pipelines](regional.qmd)

For each of the four regions (Eastern Alaska porphyry-Cu, BC Golden
Triangle multi-deposit, California Mother Lode orogenic-Au, Arizona
porphyry-Cu), I built the same kind of pipeline: pull public USGS,
NRCan, and state-survey data onto a common grid, train a Random
Forest, and hold out spatial blocks during validation so the model
can't cheat by memorizing neighbors. The chapter walks through each
region's data, the model output, and which features it leaned on
most.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Tanacross (eastern Alaska) porphyry-Cu prospectivity, random forest output](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch2_regional.png)](regional.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [3. goldbug (live tool, CA Mother Lode)](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/)

The Mother Lode model from Chapter 2 is interesting on its own,
but it doesn't tell a prospector where to actually go. goldbug
closes that gap. It takes the per-cell probabilities and filters
them to federal land that's actually open for an individual
prospector to file a notice of location on. The hard part isn't
the modeling. It's that BLM publishes the relevant data across
roughly a dozen different endpoints with inconsistent schemas,
and several of the obvious filters are subtly wrong. The chapter
has the live map embedded plus a writeup of what the tool does,
how the open-land calculation is built, and what it still doesn't
catch (historic mineral patents, split estate, placer-specific
modeling).
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![goldbug live map: topo basemap, federal gap parcels (yellow), active mining claims (red), with a parcel popup showing the score breakdown and the layer-toggle panel on the right](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch3_goldbug.png)](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [4. Reproducing + auditing two published methods](reproductions.qmd)

I cloned the official code for two well-known mineral-prospectivity
papers and re-ran them with proper validation. **Lawley 2022**
(continental Zn-Pb, published AUC 0.983) reproduces cleanly, but the
published number includes a label leak (2.4 pp), a 1-D vs 2-D
spatial-blocking gap (9.0 pp), and a cross-continent transfer
collapse (~16 pp). Properly validated, the AUC drops to 0.71-0.87.
The published code also had a performance problem: a critical piece
used an O(N²) algorithm that didn't finish after half an hour. I
rewrote it as O(N) and got the runtime down to a few minutes.
**DEEP-SEAM** (Curnamona REE, published 86% top-2% capture) is
measured under a 0-D random split with no spatial blocking at all,
and the implementation diverges from the paper text in two places. I
tried to find a charitable read of each and report what I actually
saw.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![Waterfall chart of Lawley 2022 AUC stages](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch4_reproductions.png)](reproductions.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [5. Cross-region experiments](cross_region.qmd)

I ran a five-region transferability test on the DEEP-SEAM
deviation-network architecture. The 2-3× advantage over Random
Forest that the paper reports holds on its tuning dataset (Curnamona
REE, 7 positives) and nowhere else. The most pointed test was on the
US Western Carbonatite REE Belt (14 positives, the same deposit
type as Curnamona): DEEP-SEAM still loses to Random Forest. On the
other three regions (Tanacross porphyry-Cu, Arizona porphyry-Cu,
Lawley continental MVT under 2-D blocked CV), the gap widens to 7-37
percentage points in favor of tree-based methods. Useful negative
result.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![DEEP-SEAM vs Random Forest across 5 regions](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch5_cross_region.png)](cross_region.qmd)
:::
::::

:::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-9}
### [6. Drill planning under uncertainty](drill_planning.qmd)

A prospectivity map by itself doesn't tell you where to put the next
drill hole. I built a POMDP planner with Monte Carlo Tree Search,
run on the BC Golden Triangle regional prospectivity prior and
ported from Mern et al.'s Intelligent Prospector (GMD, 2023).
Three policies side-by-side on the same prior: random, greedy, and
POMCP. POMCP gains roughly 10-15% in expected reward over greedy
at modest planner-depth budgets, more at deeper budgets. This is a
1.0 version against the v1.0 assumptions (i.i.d. Bernoulli prior,
noiseless sensor); the v2.0 extensions (correlated draws, noisy
sensors) would widen the POMCP-vs-greedy gap.
:::
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-3}
[![POMCP discovery curves on BC Golden Triangle](data/derived/portfolio_charts/thumbs/ch6_drill_planning.png)](drill_planning.qmd)
:::
::::

## Methodology

Keeping in mind that I am a programmer and not a statistician, here
is my take on methodology.

Federal and state surveys publish a lot of mineral data: occurrence
records, geologic maps, magnetics and gravity, stream-sediment
geochemistry, satellite imagery. I pull these onto a common grid and
each cell of land becomes a feature vector. I train a Random Forest
to recognize cells with known deposits, holding out spatial blocks
so the model can't cheat by memorizing neighbors. The output is a
per-cell score across the region: the model's guess at where the
unknown deposits are.

The part I've spent the most time on isn't the training, it's the
validation afterward. The methodology has four well-known traps:
inflated training positives, spatial leakage, exploration bias in
the labels, and cross-region transfer failure. All four show up in
this portfolio. Chapter 3 is the most explicit about quantifying
them, showing how much of two published AUC numbers each trap
accounts for. The same issues are flagged in the within-region work
where I noticed them, and I'm sure I missed some.

## Stack

Public data sources: USGS, NRCan, state surveys, Sentinel-2. Code is
Python: scikit-learn, pandas + pyarrow, geopandas + rasterio,
PyTorch (for the DEEP-SEAM port), PyVista for the Bear Cub 3D
visualization, Quarto for the site itself.

## Site map

| Chapter | Detail |
|---|---|
| [1. Bear Cub](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/bear_cub.html) | [main](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html), [mining_review](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/notebooks/bear_cub/mining_review.html), [Bear Cub GP](https://johnsondevco.com/bearcub/notebooks/bear_cub/gp_recommender.html) |
| [2. Regional MPM](regional.qmd) | [Eastern Alaska](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity.html), [BCGT](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/bcgt/bcgt_porphyry_prospectivity.html), [Mother Lode](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/motherlode.html), [Arizona](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/arizona/main.html) |
| [3. goldbug](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/) | [live tool](https://www.johnsondevco.com/goldbug/) |
| [4. Reproductions + audits](reproductions.qmd) | [Lawley](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/lawley.html), [Lawley audit](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/lawley_audit.html), [DEEP-SEAM](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/deep_seam_cross_region.html), [DEEP-SEAM audit](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/deep_seam_audit.html) |
| [5. Cross-region](cross_region.qmd) | [four-cell deep dive](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/cross_region/four_cell.html), [US Carbonatite REE](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/us_carbonatite_ree.html) |
| [6. Drill planning](drill_planning.qmd) | [BCGT POMDP](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html) |

## Reproduce

```bash
git clone <this-repo>
cd ai-minerals
uv sync
quarto render
```

License: MIT.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from datetime import date
print(f"\n---\n\n*Site rendered {date.today().isoformat()}.*")
```
